question: A 46-year-old woman presents with left-sided tinnitus and severe-to-profound hearing loss. MRI of the head is performed (image provided). Based on the imaging, which statement best describes the probability that a germline mutation predisposing to this lesion would be transmitted to her future children?

options.C: Low — close to general population risk

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the MRI image for lesion location and number. I see a single, well-circumscribed, enhancing rounded mass at the left cerebellopontine angle/internal auditory canal region that is causing local mass effect; this appearance is most consistent with a unilateral vestibular schwannoma given the clinical stem (left-sided tinnitus and hearing loss).

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the question, which asks about the probability that a germline mutation predisposing to this lesion would be transmitted to her children. The answer choices frame a high (~50%) risk as an autosomal dominant inherited scenario and lower choices as progressively closer to population risk.

Then, I consider imaging features that would suggest a heritable syndrome with high transmission risk. A hereditary predisposition that confers a high (≈50%) transmission probability would typically be associated with bilateral or multiple characteristic lesions. On this MRI slice there is only a unilateral lesion on the left and no obvious similar lesion on the right or additional intracranial masses visible here, so there are no imaging signs on this study to indicate a syndromic, autosomal-dominant condition.

Finally, tying these observations to the options: because the image shows an isolated, unilateral tumor without features suggestive of a hereditary syndrome, the most appropriate conclusion based on the imaging provided is that the chance of a germline mutation being present and thus being transmitted to future children is low — close to the general population risk.
caption: Patient 1: Preoperative magnetic resonance image. An axial T1-weighted image with contrast shows a classic expansile internal auditory canal and cerebellopontine angle mass consistent with left vestibular schwannoma. No vascular abnormalities are appreciated.
sub_caption: An axial T1-weighted image with contrast shows a classic expansile internal auditory canal and cerebellopontine angle mass consistent with left vestibular schwannoma. No vascular abnormalities are appreciated.
image_context: A 46-year-old woman presented with left-sided tinnitus and severe to profound hearing loss. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed an enhancing 23 × 23 × 21-mm left-sided CPA mass consistent with vestibular schwannoma (VS; <xref rid="sbsr01051-1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). Vascular anatomy appeared normal, and there was no history of coagulopathy.). Vascular anatomy appeared normal, and there was no history of coagulopathy.
relevant_image_context: A 46-year-old woman presented with left-sided tinnitus and severe to profound hearing loss. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed an enhancing 23 × 23 × 21-mm left-sided CPA mass consistent with vestibular schwannoma (VS). Vascular anatomy appeared normal, and there was no history of coagulopathy.
